bio.bio_blurb: Wife of Adelbert Ames Jr., Del's mother

notable.notable_blurb: Scientist behind the Ames room illusion

narrative_blocks.header: He built rooms that fool the eye
narrative_blocks.body: His distorted Ames room and tilting Ames window became among the most famous demonstrations in the science of perception. They showed how much the mind supplies what the eye merely seems to see.

narrative_blocks.header: From law and painting to the eye itself
narrative_blocks.body: He took a law degree at Harvard and tried painting before deciding to study vision scientifically, meaning to return to art once he understood it. The study mastered him instead, and optics became his life's work.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the Dartmouth Eye Institute
narrative_blocks.body: He founded a department of physiological optics at Dartmouth and became research director of its eye institute, recruiting designers and clinicians to study binocular vision. Rockefeller money and the optical industry sustained the work.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of a Reconstruction general and governor
narrative_blocks.body: His father had been a Union general and a Reconstruction governor and senator in Mississippi, a defender of Black suffrage in the bitter years after the war. His mother was the daughter of the controversial general Benjamin Butler.

narrative_blocks.header: His ideas reached far beyond the lab
narrative_blocks.body: His transactional theory of perception drew him into correspondence with the philosopher John Dewey and shaped a school of psychology after him. He was honored late with the optical society's highest medal.

cross_connections.display_label: married his son Del, the Harvard neuroscientist
pioneered personnel testing, born the same 1880

bio.bio_blurb: Perception scientist; father of Del Ames III

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard neuroscientist; created the Ames medium

narrative_blocks.header: The fluid that keeps brain tissue alive
narrative_blocks.body: He devised an artificial cerebrospinal fluid, the Ames medium, that lets scientists keep nervous-system tissue working for days outside the body. It remains the standard solution for the purpose today.

Working with rabbit brains, he showed that after enough minutes without blood the smallest vessels clamp shut and will not refill, the first irreversible injury of a stroke. The finding later proved true in the heart and other organs.

narrative_blocks.header: Skiing competitively into his late eighties
narrative_blocks.body: Class valedictorian at Exeter and Phi Beta Kappa at Harvard, he kept racing in masters ski events until almost ninety. He was last seen on cross-country skis at ninety-six.

cross_connections.display_label: pioneered cardiac defibrillation as a Hooker physiologist

bio.bio_blurb: Harvard neuroscientist; created the Ames medium

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the uncle lost over France
narrative_blocks.body: Quentin Roosevelt, the president's youngest son, was shot down over France in 1918. Five years later this niece was born and given his name to keep — Judith Quentin Derby.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a dynasty of scientists
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband and his father both made their names studying the brain and the eye, a rare scientific lineage. She died in 1973, in her forties, the mother of three.
